structured.summary: The paper proposes SPANDROP, a simple data augmentation technique that randomly removes spans from long input sequences to create counterfactual examples, helping models identify sparse supervision signals. A beta-Bernoulli variant is introduced to reduce distribution drift, and experiments on synthetic and real NLP tasks demonstrate improved data efficiency and generalization.

strengths.point: Novel and simple data augmentation method tailored for long sequences with sparse supervision.
strengths.evidence: Section 2 introduces SPANDROP, which randomly ablates spans without replacement, preserving relative order and avoiding position bias.

strengths.point: Theoretical analysis supporting the method and its beta-Bernoulli variant.
strengths.evidence: Section 2.2 and 2.3 provide formal definitions and theoretical properties, including probability bounds and entropy analysis.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation on both synthetic and multiple real-world NLP datasets.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 presents synthetic FINDCATS experiments; Section 4 shows results on SQuAD, MultiRC, HotpotQA, and DocRED.

strengths.point: Demonstrated improvements in sample efficiency and generalization, especially in low-data regimes.
strengths.evidence: Figure 2(a) and Figure 3 (left) show significant gains in data efficiency and performance with limited training data.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on how to optimally select span granularity for different tasks.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 notes that larger span sizes degrade performance but leaves optimal span choice to future work.

weaknesses.point: The performance gains on real-world datasets are modest compared to synthetic tasks.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 acknowledges that improvements on natural language data are less significant due to span dependencies.

weaknesses.point: The method assumes knowledge or estimation of supporting facts size (m), which may not be available in practice.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.1 and 2.2 rely on the assumption that m is known or can be estimated to set drop probabilities.

weaknesses.point: No ablation or comparison with other advanced augmentation or regularization techniques beyond masking.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments mainly compare SPANDROP variants with baseline and masking; other augmentation methods are not evaluated.

weaknesses.point: Potential computational overhead or impact on training time is not discussed.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not report training efficiency or runtime implications of applying SPANDROP.

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis of failure cases or scenarios where SPANDROP might hurt performance.
weaknesses.evidence: While some deterioration with large drop ratios is noted, detailed failure analysis is lacking.

weaknesses.point: The method is evaluated only on Transformer-based models; generality to other architectures is not shown.
weaknesses.evidence: All experiments use Transformer variants (ELECTRA, HuggingFace Transformers) without exploring other model types.

questions.question: How sensitive is SPANDROP to the choice of span size in domains other than NLP, such as time series or protein sequences?
questions.motivation: Understanding span granularity effects is important for applying the method beyond text.

questions.question: Can the authors provide guidance or heuristics for estimating the number of supporting facts (m) in real-world tasks where it is unknown?
questions.motivation: Setting the drop probability p depends on m; practical applicability requires ways to estimate or adapt to unknown m.

questions.question: Have the authors considered combining SPANDROP with other regularization or augmentation techniques, and if so, what are the interactions?
questions.motivation: Exploring complementarity with existing methods could enhance overall performance.

questions.question: What is the computational impact of applying SPANDROP during training in terms of runtime and memory?
questions.motivation: Practical deployment requires understanding any overhead introduced by the augmentation.

critique_points.point_001: Limited discussion on how to optimally select span granularity for different tasks. (Section 4 notes that larger span sizes degrade performance but leaves optimal span choice to future work.)
critique_points.point_002: The performance gains on real-world datasets are modest compared to synthetic tasks. (Section 4 acknowledges that improvements on natural language data are less significant due to span dependencies.)
critique_points.point_003: The method assumes knowledge or estimation of supporting facts size (m), which may not be available in practice. (Section 2.1 and 2.2 rely on the assumption that m is known or can be estimated to set drop probabilities.)
critique_points.point_004: No ablation or comparison with other advanced augmentation or regularization techniques beyond masking. (Experiments mainly compare SPANDROP variants with baseline and masking; other augmentation methods are not evaluated.)
critique_points.point_005: Potential computational overhead or impact on training time is not discussed. (The paper does not report training efficiency or runtime implications of applying SPANDROP.)
critique_points.point_006: Limited analysis of failure cases or scenarios where SPANDROP might hurt performance. (While some deterioration with large drop ratios is noted, detailed failure analysis is lacking.)
critique_points.point_007: The method is evaluated only on Transformer-based models; generality to other architectures is not shown. (All experiments use Transformer variants (ELECTRA, HuggingFace Transformers) without exploring other model types.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is SPANDROP to the choice of span size in domains other than NLP, such as time series or protein sequences? (Understanding span granularity effects is important for applying the method beyond text.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the authors provide guidance or heuristics for estimating the number of supporting facts (m) in real-world tasks where it is unknown? (Setting the drop probability p depends on m; practical applicability requires ways to estimate or adapt to unknown m.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered combining SPANDROP with other regularization or augmentation techniques, and if so, what are the interactions? (Exploring complementarity with existing methods could enhance overall performance.)
critique_points.point_011: What is the computational impact of applying SPANDROP during training in terms of runtime and memory? (Practical deployment requires understanding any overhead introduced by the augmentation.)
